title: Transfer Learning & Domain Adaptation
description: Fine-tuning, feature extraction, domain adaptation (supervised, unsupervised, adversarial), multi-task learning, and task arithmetic for reusing pretrained models.
details: Transfer learning enables knowledge from one task to improve learning on a related task — the dominant paradigm in modern ML (pretrained models fine-tuned for downstream tasks). This file covers inductive/transductive/unsupervised transfer learning, pretraining-finetuning pipelines (BERT, ResNet, ViT, GPT), feature extraction vs fine-tuning, progressive unfreezing, catastrophic forgetting and elastic weight consolidation, domain adaptation (supervised, unsupervised, adversarial via Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks), multi-task learning with hard/soft parameter sharing, task arithmetic for model merging, one-shot and few-shot learning with MAML and prototypical networks, and zero-shot inference. Transfer learning is the most frequently tested applied ML topic in interviews (especially for NLP/computer vision roles).

sections.title: Pretraining & Fine-tuning
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A model pretrained on a large, generic dataset learns useful features (edges in images, syntax in text). Fine-tuning adapts those features to a specific task with limited data, requiring far fewer examples than training from scratch.

Key strategies:

  • Feature extraction: Freeze the pretrained backbone (no gradient updates). Train only a new classifier head on top. Use when target data is small (< 1K examples) or very similar to pretraining domain.
  • Full fine-tuning: Update all weights with a small learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5). Use when target data is moderate (1K-100K) or domain is different.
  • Progressive unfreezing: Start with frozen backbone, train head. Then unfreeze last N layers, train. Gradually unfreeze more. Reduces catastrophic forgetting.
  • Discriminative learning rates: Lower LR for earlier layers (learned general features), higher LR for later layers (need task-specific adaptation). Typically 2.5x–10x difference.
  • Catastrophic forgetting: Fine-tuning overwrites pretrained knowledge. Mitigated by: lower LR, progressive unfreezing, EWC penalty, or replayed examples.

Common pitfalls: Using too high LR (forgets pretrained features), excessive fine-tuning on tiny datasets (overfits), not normalizing inputs the same way as pretraining, changing the input resolution or sequence length.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torchvision import models, transforms, datasets
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# === ResNet feature extraction ===
model = models.resnet50(pretrained=True)

# Freeze all layers
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Replace classifier head
num_features = model.fc.in_features
model.fc = nn.Linear(num_features, 10)  # 10 target classes

# Only head parameters are trainable
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.fc.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

# === Full fine-tuning with discriminative LR ===
model = models.resnet50(pretrained=True)
model.fc = nn.Linear(2048, 10)

# Different LRs for different layers
params = [
    {'params': model.layer1.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-6},
    {'params': model.layer2.parameters(), 'lr': 5e-6},
    {'params': model.layer3.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-5},
    {'params': model.layer4.parameters(), 'lr': 5e-5},
    {'params': model.fc.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-4},
]
optimizer = optim.Adam(params, lr=1e-5)  # default for any unlisted

# === Progressive unfreezing ===
def train_with_unfreeze(model, dataloader, epochs_per_stage=5):
    # Stage 1: train only head
    for param in model.parameters():
        param.requires_grad = False
    model.fc.requires_grad = True
    optimizer = optim.Adam(model.fc.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    train_epochs(model, dataloader, optimizer, epochs_per_stage)
    
    # Stage 2: unfreeze last block
    for param in model.layer4.parameters():
        param.requires_grad = True
    optimizer = optim.Adam([
        {'params': model.layer4.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-5},
        {'params': model.fc.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-4},
    ])
    train_epochs(model, dataloader, optimizer, epochs_per_stage)
    
    # Stage 3: full fine-tuning
    for param in model.parameters():
        param.requires_grad = True
    optimizer = optim.Adam([
        {'params': model.layer1.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-6},
        {'params': model.layer2.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-6},
        {'params': model.layer3.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-5},
        {'params': model.layer4.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-5},
        {'params': model.fc.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-4},
    ])
    train_epochs(model, dataloader, optimizer, epochs_per_stage)

# === Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) ===
def ewc_loss(model, fisher, old_params, new_loss, lambda_ewc=100):
    """Penalize changes to important weights."""
    ewc = 0
    for name, param in model.named_parameters():
        if name in fisher:
            ewc += (fisher[name] * (param - old_params[name])**2).sum()
    return new_loss + lambda_ewc * ewc

# Fisher = E[ (d log p(y|x, theta) / d theta)^2 ]
# Computed from gradient of pretrained model's likelihood

sections.title: Domain Adaptation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A model trained on source domain (e.g., synthetic images) fails on target domain (real photos) due to distribution shift. Domain adaptation aligns the distributions so the model generalizes to the target.

Types of domain adaptation:

  • Supervised: Labeled data in both source and target (few target labels). Fine-tune on target labels.
  • Semi-supervised: Labeled source + unlabeled target + few labeled target.
  • Unsupervised (UDA): Labeled source + unlabeled target only. Most common and hardest.

UDA techniques:

  • Adversarial DA (DANN): Train feature extractor to fool a domain classifier. Feature extractor learns domain-invariant features. The gradient reversal layer flips gradients to make features indistinguishable between domains.
  • CORAL (CORrelation ALignment): Align second-order statistics (covariance) of source and target features. Simple, effective for small distribution shifts.
  • Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD): Minimize distribution distance in RKHS. MMD = ||E[φ(Xs)] - E[φ(Xt)]||. Often added as a regularization term.
  • Self-training: Train on labeled source, predict pseudo-labels on target, re-train with confident predictions. Used in FixMatch, Noisy Student.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# === Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN) ===

class GradientReversal(torch.autograd.Function):
    """Forward as identity, backward flips gradient sign."""
    @staticmethod
    def forward(ctx, x, lambda_):
        ctx.lambda_ = lambda_
        return x.view_as(x)
    
    @staticmethod
    def backward(ctx, grad_output):
        return -ctx.lambda_ * grad_output, None

class DANN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, feature_dim=512, num_classes=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.feature_extractor = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 64, 3, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1),
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(64, feature_dim)
        )
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(feature_dim, num_classes)
        self.domain_discriminator = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(feature_dim, 128),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, 1),
            nn.Sigmoid()
        )
        self.grl = GradientReversal.apply
    
    def forward(self, x, lambda_=0.5):
        features = self.feature_extractor(x)
        # Label prediction
        class_out = self.classifier(features)
        # Domain prediction with gradient reversal
        domain_out = self.domain_discriminator(self.grl(features, lambda_))
        return class_out, domain_out

# Training alternates:
# 1. Minimize classifier loss on labeled source
# 2. Minimize domain discriminator loss (source=1, target=0)
# 3. Feature extractor tries to maximize domain loss (via GRL)

model = DANN()
source_loader = None  # labeled source

def train_dann(model, source_loader, target_loader, epochs=50):
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
    class_loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
    domain_loss_fn = nn.BCELoss()
    
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        # Progressively increase lambda (from 0 to 1)
        p = epoch / epochs
        lambda_ = 2.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-10.0 * p)) - 1.0
        
        for (src_x, src_y), (tgt_x, _) in zip(source_loader, target_loader):
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            
            # Source: predict labels + domain = 1
            class_out, domain_out = model(src_x, lambda_)
            class_loss = class_loss_fn(class_out, src_y)
            domain_loss = domain_loss_fn(domain_out.squeeze(), 
                                         torch.ones(src_x.size(0)))
            
            # Target: only domain = 0
            _, domain_out_t = model(tgt_x, lambda_)
            domain_loss += domain_loss_fn(domain_out_t.squeeze(),
                                          torch.zeros(tgt_x.size(0)))
            
            total_loss = class_loss + domain_loss
            total_loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()

# === CORAL (Correlation Alignment) ===
def coral_loss(source_features, target_features):
    """Minimize distance between covariance matrices."""
    def covariance(x):
        n = x.size(0)
        x_centered = x - x.mean(dim=0)
        return (x_centered.T @ x_centered) / (n - 1)
    
    cov_s = covariance(source_features)
    cov_t = covariance(target_features)
    return (cov_s - cov_t).pow(2).sum() / (4 * source_features.size(1)**2)

sections.title: Multi-task Learning & Task Arithmetic
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Multi-task learning trains a single model on multiple related objectives. Learning A helps learning B because shared features are reinforced across tasks. Task arithmetic lets you combine or subtract model weights to compose capabilities post-hoc.

MTL architectures:

  • Hard parameter sharing: Shared backbone + task-specific heads. Most common. Reduces overfitting by forcing shared representations. Risk: task conflict (different tasks pull gradients in opposing directions).
  • Soft parameter sharing: Each task has its own network with regularized distances between params (e.g., L2, trace norm). More flexible, more parameters.
  • Cross-stitch networks: Learnable linear combinations of task-specific representations. Each layer learns how much to share.
  • Mixture of Experts (MoE): Multiple expert subnetworks + a gating network that routes each input to the best expert(s). Each task can use different experts.

Task balancing: Different tasks have different loss scales. Without balancing, one task dominates. Solutions:

  • Uncertainty weighting: Weight each task loss by learned variance (lower variance = higher weight). Loss = Σ (Lt / (2σt²) + log σt).
  • Gradient surgery (PCGrad): If task gradients conflict (cosine < 0), project each gradient onto the normal plane of the other. Eliminates conflicting components.
  • Dynamic weight averaging: Adjust weights based on loss ratio. wt = exp(−Lt / T) / Σ exp(−Lt / T).

Task arithmetic (model merging):

  • Train separate fine-tuned models for different tasks on a base model.
  • Compute Δ = fine-tuned - base for each task.
  • Combine task vectors: θmerged = θbase + Σ λt Δt.
  • Negative task vector = forgetting capability: θunlearn = θbase - λ Δt.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# === Hard parameter sharing MTL ===
class SharedBackboneMTL(nn.Module):
    """Single backbone, multiple task heads."""
    def __init__(self, input_dim=128, num_tasks=5, hidden_dim=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.shared = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
        )
        # Task-specific heads
        self.task_heads = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1) for _ in range(num_tasks)
        ])
    
    def forward(self, x, task_id):
        shared = self.shared(x)
        return self.task_heads[task_id](shared)

# === Uncertainty weighting (automatically balance tasks) ===
class UncertainMTL(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, backbone, num_tasks):
        super().__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone
        self.task_heads = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Linear(64, 1) for _ in range(num_tasks)
        ])
        # Learnable log variance for each task (lower = more weight)
        self.log_var = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(num_tasks))
    
    def forward(self, x, task_id):
        features = self.backbone(x)
        pred = self.task_heads[task_id](features)
        return pred
    
    def multi_task_loss(self, preds, targets, task_ids):
        """
        L = sum_t (L_t / (2 * sigma_t^2) + log(sigma_t))
        The log(sigma_t) term prevents sigma_t from going to infinity.
        """
        total_loss = 0
        for pred, target, task_id in zip(preds, targets, task_ids):
            mse = (pred - target).pow(2).mean()
            precision = torch.exp(-self.log_var[task_id])
            loss = precision * mse + 0.5 * self.log_var[task_id]
            total_loss += loss
        return total_loss

# === Task arithmetic (model merging) ===
# After fine-tuning on N tasks:
# base = base_model.state_dict()
# ft_tasks = [task1_state, task2_state, ...]
# task_vectors = [{k: ft[k] - base[k] for k in base} for ft in ft_tasks]
#
# # Add capabilities
# merged = {k: base[k] + sum(tv[k] for tv in task_vectors) for k in base}
#
# # Forgetting capability (negative task vector)
# unlearned = {k: base[k] - 0.5 * task_vectors[0][k] for k in base}
#
# Useful for: removing copyrighted content, debiasing, unlearning

# === Cross-stitch network ===
def cross_stitch_unit(x1, x2, alpha):
    """
    x1, x2: representations from task 1 and 2
    alpha: 2x2 learnable matrix
    Returns: mixed representations
    """
    batch_size = x1.size(0)
    combined = torch.stack([x1, x2], dim=1)  # [B, 2, D]
    # alpha: [2, 2] -> mix coefficients
    mixed = torch.matmul(alpha, combined.permute(1, 0, 2))
    return mixed[0], mixed[1]  # x1_mixed, x2_mixed

sections.title: Few-shot Learning & Meta-learning
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Humans learn new concepts from 1–5 examples. Few-shot learning enables ML models to do the same by learning a good initialization (MAML) or learning a good similarity metric (ProtoNets).

Problem setting:

  • N-way, K-shot: N classes, K examples per class in support set. Query set contains additional examples to classify.
  • Episodic training: Each episode samples N classes, K support + Q query examples. Train on many episodes to generalize to new classes.

Approaches:

  • Metric-based (Prototypical Networks): Compute prototype = mean of support embeddings per class. Classify query by nearest prototype (Euclidean or cosine distance). Simple, strong baseline.
  • Optimization-based (MAML): Learn initialization θ such that one (or few) gradient steps on K examples yields good generalization. Outer loop: meta-update θ based on query loss after inner loop adaptation. MAML requires second-order gradients (Hessian-vector products).
  • Model-based (Memory Augmented): Network with external memory (NTM, DNC). Writes support examples to memory, reads relevant info during query. End-to-end differentiable.
  • Finetuning baseline: Just fine-tune a pretrained model on the N × K examples. Often competitive with dedicated few-shot methods when using a strong pretrained backbone (e.g., CLIP).
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import numpy as np

# === Prototypical Networks ===
class PrototypicalNet(nn.Module):
    """Classify query by nearest class prototype."""
    def __init__(self, backbone):
        super().__init__()
        self.backbone = backbone
    
    def forward(self, support, query, n_way, k_shot):
        """
        support: [N*K, C, H, W]
        query: [N*Q, C, H, W]
        """
        # Embed all examples
        sup_emb = self.backbone(support)  # [N*K, D]
        qry_emb = self.backbone(query)    # [N*Q, D]
        
        # Compute prototypes (mean per class)
        prototypes = sup_emb.view(n_way, k_shot, -1).mean(dim=1)  # [N, D]
        
        # Euclidean distance from query to each prototype
        dists = torch.cdist(qry_emb, prototypes)  # [N*Q, N]
        
        # Negative distance as logits
        return -dists

# === MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) ===
class MAML:
    """Learn initialization for fast adaptation."""
    def __init__(self, model, inner_lr=0.01, meta_lr=0.001):
        self.model = model
        self.inner_lr = inner_lr
        self.meta_optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=meta_lr)
    
    def inner_loop(self, x_support, y_support, inner_steps=5):
        """Adapt initialization to task with few gradient steps."""
        # Clone model with gradient tracking
        adapted = {name: param.clone() for name, param in self.model.named_parameters()}
        
        for _ in range(inner_steps):
            # Forward pass with adapted params
            pred = F.cross_entropy(
                self._forward_with_params(x_support, adapted),
                y_support
            )
            # Manual gradient computation
            grads = torch.autograd.grad(pred, adapted.values(), create_graph=True)
            for (name, _), grad in zip(adapted.items(), grads):
                adapted[name] = adapted[name] - self.inner_lr * grad
        
        return adapted
    
    def _forward_with_params(self, x, params):
        """Forward pass with custom parameters."""
        # Simplified: assume model is nn.Sequential
        h = x
        for name, layer in self.model.named_children():
            if isinstance(layer, nn.Linear):
                w = params.get(f'{name}.weight', layer.weight)
                b = params.get(f'{name}.bias', layer.bias)
                h = F.linear(h, w, b)
            elif isinstance(layer, nn.ReLU):
                h = F.relu(h)
        return h
    
    def meta_step(self, task_batch):
        """
        task_batch: list of (x_support, y_support, x_query, y_query)
        """
        meta_loss = 0
        for x_sup, y_sup, x_qry, y_qry in task_batch:
            # Inner loop: adapt
            adapted = self.inner_loop(x_sup, y_sup)
            
            # Outer loop: evaluate on query
            pred = self._forward_with_params(x_qry, adapted)
            meta_loss += F.cross_entropy(pred, y_qry)
        
        meta_loss /= len(task_batch)
        
        # Meta-gradient update
        self.meta_optimizer.zero_grad()
        meta_loss.backward()
        self.meta_optimizer.step()
        return meta_loss.item()
